core_engine: labor conditions as plot — calendar, badge, role eating the person

chapter_1_beats.beat_text: a corporate stairwell between floors where the elevators don't stop, well past the hour the floor was built for. Suni Okafor is still here, the way the role is always still here. On the monitor, the same task list refreshes; the top item has been the top item for three days.

An email arrives addressed to the job title, not the name. Suni Okafor answers as the title — clipped, efficient, capable. The reply is perfect. The hands that typed it are shaking very slightly and Suni Okafor does not look at them.

the temp who has worked at forty companies and is unimpressed by all of them crosses the floor with a mop and a slow, unhurried gait, and pauses at the desk. 'They've had that chair filled for eleven years,' they say, not unkindly. 'Different names. Same hours. The chair doesn't notice when one of you leaves.'

Suni Okafor laughs it off and reaches for the next task — but the cursor blinks and nothing in them moves to meet it. The task is urgent. The urgency arrives at a body that has run out of the thing urgency is supposed to spend.

The overhead lights reach their timer and click off in a long row, leaving only the monitor glow. Suni Okafor sits inside the dim, not working, not leaving. For one held breath, no one is asking the role for anything. The silence has a shape, and the shape is shaped like a person.

Suni Okafor lifts the lanyard from around their neck — the badge, the photo, the printed title — and sets it on the desk beside the keyboard. The weight leaving the collarbone is almost nothing. It is also the first thing they have felt in weeks.

They look at the badge lying there, separate now: a name and a function, no longer the same object. 'You can stay,' Suni Okafor says quietly, to the badge, not to themselves. 'I'm going home.' The role keeps its perfect posture on the desk. The person stands up.

the temp who has worked at forty companies and is unimpressed by all of them holds the stairwell door without a word as Suni Okafor passes through it, coat over one arm, badge left behind for the morning. The to-do list is still unfinished on a dark screen above them. It will keep. So, it turns out, will they.

Next morning, the floor fills, the lights snap on, the role is picked up and worn again — but Suni Okafor arrives carrying coffee for two and stops at the temp who has worked at forty companies and is unimpressed by all of them's cart to say good morning by name. Same desk. Same hours. A person sits down in the chair this time.
